questions.question: What is the purpose of generic constraints using `extends`?

choices.a: They force generic types to extend a class at runtime
choices.b: They restrict what types can be substituted for a type parameter, allowing safe access to specific properties/methods
choices.c: They create inheritance between generic types
choices.d: They are only used in generic classes, not functions

questions.explanation:
Without constraints, a generic type parameter `T` is essentially `unknown`, so you can't access any properties on it:

```typescript
function getLength<T>(x: T): number {
  return x.length; // Error: Property 'length' does not exist on type 'T'
}
```

With a constraint:
```typescript
function getLength<T extends { length: number }>(x: T): number {
  return x.length; // OK — T is guaranteed to have .length
}
getLength('hello');    // OK
getLength([1, 2, 3]);  // OK
getLength(42);         // Error — number has no .length
```

Constraints preserve the specific type while ensuring properties exist. `T extends SomeType` means T must be assignable to SomeType.

questions.question: What does `keyof T` return?

choices.a: An array of the keys of T at runtime
choices.b: A union of all string/symbol literal types corresponding to the keys of type T at the type level
choices.d: The number of properties in T

questions.explanation:
`keyof T` produces a UNION TYPE of all the property names (keys) of type T:

```typescript
type User = { id: number; name: string; email: string };
type UserKeys = keyof User; // 'id' | 'name' | 'email'
```

This is essential for building type-safe property access utilities:

```typescript
function getProperty<T, K extends keyof T>(obj: T, key: K): T[K] {
  return obj[key]; // TypeScript knows the return type is T[K]
}

const user = { id: 1, name: 'Alice' };
const name = getProperty(user, 'name'); // type: string
const id = getProperty(user, 'id');     // type: number
getProperty(user, 'missing');           // Error!
```

`keyof` works in concert with indexed access types (`T[K]`) to give you full type inference.

questions.question: What is a conditional type and what does `infer` do inside one?

choices.a: A conditional type is a ternary at runtime; `infer` extracts a value
choices.b: A conditional type is `T extends U ? X : Y` evaluated at compile time; `infer` declares a type variable to capture a type that TypeScript infers in the checked position
choices.c: `infer` is only valid in `ReturnType` and `Parameters` built-in types
choices.d: Conditional types and `infer` are only available in TypeScript 5+

questions.explanation:
CONDITIONAL TYPES work like ternary operators for types:
```typescript
type IsString<T> = T extends string ? 'yes' : 'no';
type A = IsString<string>; // 'yes'
type B = IsString<number>; // 'no'
```

`infer` lets you CAPTURE a type in the `extends` check position:
```typescript
type ReturnType<T> = T extends (...args: any[]) => infer R ? R : never;
// If T is a function, R captures the return type; otherwise never.

type UnpackPromise<T> = T extends Promise<infer V> ? V : T;
type N = UnpackPromise<Promise<number>>; // number
type S = UnpackPromise<string>;          // string (not wrapped)
```

`infer` only works inside the `extends` clause of a conditional type. It is how all built-in utility types like `ReturnType` and `Parameters` are implemented.

questions.question: What is a mapped type?

choices.a: A type that iterates over array elements
choices.b: A type that transforms each property of an existing type using `[K in keyof T]` syntax
choices.c: A higher-order function at the type level
choices.d: A way to create dictionary types with `Record`

questions.question: What is a template literal type?

choices.a: A runtime template string that TypeScript validates
choices.b: A type that uses backtick syntax to combine and transform string literal types at the type level
choices.c: A string type that requires template literal syntax at runtime
choices.d: Template literal types are only usable with `as const`

questions.question: What are generic default type parameters (TypeScript 2.3+)?

choices.a: A way to provide a fallback type when no type argument is passed to a generic
choices.b: A way to set default VALUES for generic class properties
choices.c: Default type params are only usable in class declarations
choices.d: They define the base type constraint, same as `extends`

questions.explanation:
Generic default type parameters let you specify FALLBACK TYPES:

```typescript
interface Container<T = string> {
  value: T;
}

const c1: Container = { value: 'hello' };     // T defaults to string
const c2: Container<number> = { value: 42 };  // T explicitly set to number
```

Useful for:
• Generic React components where you want a default type for optional props
• Library types with sensible defaults
• Reducing verbosity when one type is almost always the same

Combined with constraints:
```typescript
function createStore<S = {}, A extends Action = Action>() { ... }
// Can call as createStore() or createStore<MyState>() or createStore<MyState, MyAction>()
```

questions.question: How do you distribute a conditional type over a union?

choices.a: Use `extends` with a union type — it automatically distributes
choices.c: Wrap the type parameter in `[]` to prevent distribution, or rely on automatic distribution over naked type parameters

questions.explanation:
When a conditional type has a NAKED TYPE PARAMETER (not wrapped), it AUTOMATICALLY DISTRIBUTES over unions:

```typescript
type ToArray<T> = T extends any ? T[] : never;
type R = ToArray<string | number>; // string[] | number[] (distributed!)
```

To PREVENT distribution, wrap the type in `[]`:
```typescript
type ToArrayND<T> = [T] extends [any] ? T[] : never;
type R = ToArrayND<string | number>; // (string | number)[] (not distributed)
```

Built-in utility types use this:
```typescript
type Exclude<T, U> = T extends U ? never : T;  // distributed
type Exclude<string | number | boolean, number>  // string | boolean
```

Distribution is powerful but sometimes surprising — knowing when to use `[T]` vs `T` is an advanced TypeScript skill.

questions.question: What is the `infer` keyword used for inside `extends` of conditional types?

choices.a: It infers the runtime type of a value
choices.b: It declares a fresh type variable that TypeScript fills in by pattern-matching the type structure being checked
choices.c: It is a constraint that requires the type to be inferrable
